title: Object Navigation
description: Learn how to explore and interact with controls that cannot be reached with the Tab key, using NVDA's object navigation commands.

steps.instruction: Welcome to the Object Navigation chapter. Most of what you have learned so far uses the Tab key and arrow keys to move through a screen. But some programs — especially older desktop applications — have controls that Tab cannot reach. NVDA has a separate system for exploring these called object navigation. It lets you move through every element on the screen, whether or not it accepts keyboard focus. Press Enter to continue.

NVDA thinks of the screen as a tree of objects. There is a top-level object, which might be the desktop or the main application window. Inside it are child objects — toolbars, panels, menus, and controls. Each object may contain more children. Object navigation lets you move through this tree in any direction: to the next or previous sibling, up to the parent, or down to the first child. Press Enter to continue.

There are two keyboard layouts in NVDA: desktop and laptop. Desktop layout uses the numeric keypad on the right side of a full-size keyboard. Laptop layout uses combinations of NVDA plus letter keys so you do not need a numpad. All object navigation commands in this chapter will show both layouts. If you are not sure which layout you are using, open the NVDA menu with NVDA+N, go to Preferences, then Settings, then Keyboard, and listen for the layout setting. Press Enter to continue.

lessons.title: Move to Next and Previous Object

steps.instruction: [NVDA command] To move to the next object at the same level, press NVDA+NumPad6 on desktop layout, or NVDA+Right Arrow on laptop layout. To move to the previous object, press NVDA+NumPad4 on desktop, or NVDA+Left Arrow on laptop. NVDA announces each object as you land on it. Press Enter to continue.

Try it now. Hold your NVDA key and press NumPad6 (desktop) or Right Arrow (laptop) to move to the next object. NVDA will announce what it is. Then hold NVDA and press NumPad4 (desktop) or Left Arrow (laptop) to move to the previous object. Try moving through a few objects in any open application to get the feel of it. Press Enter here when you are ready to continue.

Notice that NVDA announces the name, type, and sometimes the value of each object — similar to what NVDA+Tab tells you about a focused control, but for any object on screen whether or not it has keyboard focus. This is especially useful in programs that do not respond to the Tab key. Press Enter to continue.

lessons.title: Move to Parent and First Child

steps.instruction: [NVDA command] To move up to the parent object (the container that holds the current object), press NVDA+NumPad8 on desktop layout, or NVDA+Up Arrow on laptop layout. To move down to the first child object inside the current one, press NVDA+NumPad2 on desktop, or NVDA+Down Arrow on laptop. This is how you explore the object tree in depth. Press Enter to continue.

Try it now. Hold NVDA and press NumPad8 (desktop) or Up Arrow (laptop) to move to the parent of the current object. NVDA will announce the parent. Then press NVDA+NumPad2 (desktop) or NVDA+Down Arrow (laptop) to move back down to the first child. Exploring up and down the tree helps you find controls that are grouped together in panels or toolbars. Press Enter when you are ready to continue.

[NVDA command] When you have navigated to an object, you can have NVDA read it in different ways. Press NVDA+NumPad5 (desktop) or NVDA+Shift+, (laptop — that is NVDA, Shift, and the comma key) to hear the full description of the current navigator object. Press it twice quickly to spell out the object name. This is your primary way to examine any object you have landed on. Press Enter to continue.

Try it now. Navigate to any object using NVDA+NumPad6 or NVDA+Right Arrow. Then press NVDA+NumPad5 (desktop) or NVDA+Shift+Comma (laptop) to hear the full description of that object. Press it twice quickly to spell the name character by character. When you are ready, press Enter to continue.

lessons.title: Move Keyboard Focus to a Navigator Object

steps.instruction: [NVDA command] Once you have used object navigation to find a control, you can move keyboard focus to it so you can interact with it normally. Press NVDA+Shift+NumPadMinus (desktop) or NVDA+Shift+BackSpace (laptop) to route focus to the current navigator object. If the object can accept focus, your keyboard focus will move to it and you can then press Enter, Space, or type as normal. Press Enter to continue.

There is also a reverse command: NVDA+NumPadMinus (desktop) or NVDA+BackSpace (laptop) moves the navigator object to wherever your keyboard focus currently is. This is useful when you want to start exploring from where you already are in the program, rather than starting from the top of the object tree. Press Enter to continue.

Object navigation takes practice. The best way to get comfortable with it is to open a familiar program — like Notepad or File Explorer — and spend a few minutes exploring its object tree. Move to siblings, go up to parents, come back down to children, and read each object as you go. Over time, you will build a mental picture of how programs are structured. Press Enter to continue.

Object navigation is most useful in three situations. First, when a program has controls that Tab cannot reach — some toolbars, custom panels, and application-specific controls only show up in the object tree. Second, when you need to read something on screen that is not focused — like a status bar showing progress or a notification area. Third, when you want to explore the structure of a new program before deciding how to interact with it. Press Enter to continue.

Most everyday tasks — email, web browsing, word processing — do not require object navigation. Tab, arrow keys, and browse mode cover almost everything. Object navigation is an advanced tool you reach for when the standard methods fall short. Knowing it is there means no control on any screen is truly out of reach. Press Enter to continue.

You have completed the Object Navigation chapter! You now know how to move through the object tree in any direction, read and examine objects, and route keyboard focus to any control on screen. When you are ready, press NVDA+Shift+C to open the lesson picker and continue to the next chapter. Press Enter to finish.
